author: The Third Ecumenical Council: The Council of Ephesus
book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Third Ecumenical Council: The Council of Ephesus.
The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.

metadata.author: The Third Ecumenical Council: The Council of Ephesus

title: Canon III.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: If any of the city or country clergy have been inhibited by Nestorius or his followers from the exercise of the priesthood, on account of their orthodoxy, we have declared it just that these should be restored to their proper rank.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: And in general we forbid all the clergy who adhere to the Orthodox and Ecumenical Synod in any way to submit to the bishops who have already apostatized or shall hereafter apostatize.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: To whom Nestorius forbids the priesthood, he is most worthy; but whom he approves is profane.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: It would seem from this canon that any bishop who had become a member of the Conciliabulum of John, was considered as eo ipso having lost all jurisdiction.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: Also it would seem that the clergy were to disregard the inhibition of Nestorian prelates or at least these inhibitions were by some one to be removed.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: This principle, if generally applied, would seem to be somewhat revolutionary.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: Ad Rom. i., Vol. II., Sec.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: The words χῶρος (“place”), χώρα (“country”), and χωρίον (“district”), may be distinguished as implying locality, extension, and limitation, respectively.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: The last word commonly denotes either “an estate, a farm,” or “a fastness, a stronghold,” or (as a mathematical term) “an area.”

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: Here, as not unfrequently in later writers, it is “a region, a district,” but the same fundamental idea is presumed.

verses.book: The Canons of the Two Hundred Holy and Blessed Fathers Who Met at Ephesus.
verses.text: The relation of χῶρος to χωρίον is the same as that of ἄργυρος, χρυσός to ἀργύριον, χρυσίον, the former being the metals themselves, the latter the metals worked up into bullion or coins or plate or trinkets or images, e.g. Macar.
